Home·Journal·The coast

Our quiet plan to clean up the bay

Mia Daniels · Owner22 May 20265 min read

A clear blue sea isn't an accident. Behind every postcard view is a lot of unglamorous work — and most of ours happens before the first guest is even awake.

When my family reopened Oceans View, we made ourselves one promise: leave this beach better than we found it. It's the view we sell, after all. It seemed only fair to look after it. Twelve years on, that promise has turned into a hundred small, boring, important habits — none of them dramatic, all of them adding up.

Running on sunshine

The roof you can't see from your room is covered in solar panels. On a normal Cape summer day, the resort runs almost entirely on sunshine — the pools, the kitchens, the air-conditioning that makes a heatwave bearable. It cost us a small fortune and it was worth every cent, both for the coast and, frankly, for the power cuts.

The unglamorous list

There's no single big gesture here, just a long list of small ones we refuse to skip. The least exciting paragraph in this whole journal is also the one I'm proudest of:

  • No single-use plastic anywhere on the property — not in the rooms, not at the bar.
  • Glass water bottles, refilled from our own filtered supply.
  • A staff beach clean on the first Saturday of every month, rain or shine.
  • Greywater from the showers keeps the gardens green through the dry season.
"We sell the view. The least we can do is protect it."— Mia Daniels

Our stretch of sand has flown the Blue Flag — the international mark for a clean, safe, well-run beach — for ten years running. That's not us; that's the lifeguards, the municipality, the volunteers, and a whole community that decided this coast was worth the effort. We're just one small part of it, doing our share.

None of this changes your holiday in any way you'd notice, and that's rather the point. You came for the clear water and the clean sand. Keeping them that way is simply our half of the deal.

MD
Mia Daniels
Owner · Oceans View
